---
title: "Plant Immune Trafficking Observatory"
subtitle: "Interactive kinetic simulation of receptor trafficking, autophagy flux, and AP-4 cargo dynamics"
author: "chrissi m."
date: last-modified
format:
  html:
    embed-resources: true
    theme: cosmo
    toc: true
    page-layout: full
---

<!-- SOURCE: 20260125_ClaudeAP4/immune_dashboard.ojs + AP4_TEPSIN_expanded_analysis.md -->

## Background

Potyvirus infection occurs against the backdrop of highly dynamic plant immune
receptor trafficking. Understanding how the vesicle trafficking network responds
to infection requires integrating three interconnected pathways:

**AP-4 complex (Adaptor Protein Complex 4)** is a heterotetrameric TGN-resident
complex (subunits AP4B, AP4E, AP4M, AP4S) that mediates clathrin-independent
trafficking from the *trans*-Golgi network to endosomes. Unlike AP-1/2/3, AP-4
operates without a clathrin coat. In *Arabidopsis* it is experimentally
established to sort cargo bearing tyrosine-based (YXXΦ) motifs — including
**VACUOLAR SORTING RECEPTOR1** and the borate transporter BOR1 — via its µ
(AP4M) subunit [@fuji2016], and AP-4 subunit interactions have been mapped
systematically [@wang2022ap]; AP-4 deficiency also abolishes the hypersensitive
cell-death response to avirulent bacteria, linking it to immunity [@fuji2016].
Its most conserved cargo is **ATG9**, a lipid scramblase essential for
autophagosome biogenesis [@huang2026].

::: {.callout-warning appearance="simple"}
By contrast, the assignment of the immune regulators **NPR1** and **CBP60g** as
AP-4 cargo — and the idea of AP-4-dependent NPR1 "endosomal processing" before
nuclear import — are **predictions inferred from mammalian AP-4 biology and
sorting-motif analysis, not established plant facts**. In particular, **no direct
experimental AP-4–NPR1 interaction is documented**, so no such edge is drawn in
the protein-interaction network (Chapter 6); these links are included here only
as explicit, testable hypotheses (lower confidence than the ATG9/VSR1 cargo) and
are flagged as *predicted* wherever they appear.
:::

**ESCRT pathway** (Endosomal Sorting Complexes Required for Transport)
mediates ubiquitin-dependent sorting of activated immune receptors (e.g. FLS2,
EFR) from early endosomes into intraluminal vesicles (ILVs) of multivesicular
bodies (MVBs), ultimately targeting them for vacuolar degradation. ESCRT-I
mutation causes FLS2 mis-sorting and attenuated PTI [@spallek2013]. Phosphoinositide
PI(3)P on early endosomes and PI(3,5)P₂ on late endosomes/MVBs coordinate ESCRT
recruitment and MVB maturation [@simon2016].

**Selective autophagy** provides an alternative degradation route and is
functionally connected to ESCRT via shared PI(3)P pools and NBR1-mediated
cargo recruitment. Autophagy is required for both immune amplification (clearing
protein aggregates, eliminating pathogens) and immune termination (receptor
turnover) [@ustun2018; @leary2018].

The simulation below models steady-state receptor pool dynamics across all three
pathways, allowing in silico perturbation of individual components to predict
pathological outcomes.

## Interactive Simulation

<!-- Rewritten as pure OJS — React/recharts version is not compatible with OJS runtime -->

```{ojs}
//| echo: false

// ============================================================================
// STATIC DATA
// ============================================================================

genotype = [
  { label: "None (WT)",                  value: "none" },
  { label: "AP-4 knockout",              value: "ap4_knockout" },
  { label: "ESCRT knockout",             value: "escrt_knockout" },
  { label: "ATG5 knockout",              value: "atg5_knockout" },
  { label: "VPS4-DN",                    value: "vps4_dominant_negative" },
  { label: "Bafilomycin (autophagy inh)", value: "bafilomycin" },
  { label: "PI3K inhibitor",             value: "pi3k_inhibitor" },
  { label: "Sterol depletion",           value: "sterol_depletion" },
  { label: "AP-4 + ESCRT double KO",     value: "ap4_escrt_double" },
  { label: "AP-4-DN",                    value: "ap4_dominant_negative" }
]

resptype = [
  { label: "PTI (Pattern-Triggered Immunity)", value: "pti" },
  { label: "ETI (Effector-Triggered Immunity)", value: "eti" },
  { label: "Mixed",                            value: "mixed" }
]

pathotype = [
  { label: "None",                          value: "none" },
  { label: "Magnaporthe oryzae (fungus)",   value: "magnaporthe" },
  { label: "Colletotrichum (fungus)",       value: "colletotrichum" },
  { label: "Botrytis cinerea (fungus)",     value: "botrytis" },
  { label: "Fusarium graminearum (fungus)", value: "fusarium" },
  { label: "Potyvirus (RNA virus)",         value: "potyvirus" }
]

// Short profiles shown in the pathogen card. Virus effects on the trafficking
// network are those documented for potyviruses (autophagy manipulation, ER/6K2
// membrane remodelling co-opting endomembrane flux); modifiers are illustrative.
pathogenInfo = ({
  none:          { kind: "—",      note: "No pathogen challenge." },
  magnaporthe:   { kind: "Fungus", note: "Hemibiotroph; appressorial penetration; strong chitin-triggered ROS." },
  colletotrichum:{ kind: "Fungus", note: "Hemibiotroph; effectors target autophagy and SA signalling." },
  botrytis:      { kind: "Fungus", note: "Necrotroph; oxalic acid + ROS drive host cell death." },
  fusarium:      { kind: "Fungus", note: "Cell-wall degradation and mycotoxins; moderate ROS." },
  potyvirus:     { kind: "RNA virus", note: "6K2/VPg remodel ER membranes; induces/co-opts autophagy and endomembrane flux for replication (HC-Pro suppresses RNA silencing)." }
})
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
//| panel: input

viewof perturbation = Inputs.select(genotype, {
  label: "Genotypic perturbation",
  format: x => x.label,
  value: genotype.find(g => g.value === "none")
})

viewof response = Inputs.select(resptype, {
  label: "Response type",
  format: x => x.label,
  value: resptype.find(g => g.value === "pti")
})

viewof pathogen = Inputs.select(pathotype, {
  label: "Pathogen",
  format: x => x.label,
  value: pathotype.find(g => g.value === "fusarium")
})

viewof timeMax = Inputs.range([10, 600], { value: 120, step: 5, label: "Max simulation time (min)" })

viewof isRunningRaw = Inputs.checkbox(["▶  Run simulation"])
isRunning = isRunningRaw.length > 0
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
// Pathogen profile card
html`<div style="background:#fff7ed; border:1px solid #fdba74; border-radius:8px;
                 padding:10px 14px; margin:6px 0; font-size:13px;">
  <b style="color:#c2410c;">Pathogen:</b>
  <span style="color:#7c2d12;">${pathogen.label}</span>
  <span style="color:#9a3412;"> · ${pathogenInfo[pathogen.value].kind}</span>
  <div style="color:#57534e; margin-top:4px;">${pathogenInfo[pathogen.value].note}</div>
</div>`
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false

// ============================================================================
// PERTURBATION MODIFIERS
// ============================================================================

function getPerturbationModifiers(type) {
  const m = {
    endocytosis: 1, ubiquitination: 1, escrt: 1, autophagy: 1,
    recycling: 1, fusion: 1, lipids: 1, ros: 1, cellWall: 1,
    ap4Sorting: 1, npr1Nuclear: 1, cbp60gNuclear: 1, exosomeTopology: 0
  };
  switch (type) {
    case "ap4_knockout":
      // Indirect topology risk only (autophagy→ESCRT stress); AP-4 is not in the
      // scission machinery.
      m.ap4Sorting = 0.05; m.autophagy = 0.6; m.npr1Nuclear = 0.5; m.exosomeTopology = 0.15; break;
    case "escrt_knockout":
      m.escrt = 0.05; m.autophagy = 1.3; m.ros = 1.2; m.exosomeTopology = 0.12; break;
    case "atg5_knockout":
      m.autophagy = 0.05; m.escrt = 1.3; m.ros = 0.9; break;
    case "vps4_dominant_negative":
      // Direct ESCRT-III scission defect — mechanistically the strongest single
      // driver of topology reversal.
      m.escrt = 0.1; m.lipids = 0.6; m.ros = 1.4; m.exosomeTopology = 0.35; break;
    case "bafilomycin":
      // Blocks vacuolar fusion/acidification (degradation), not autophagosome
      // formation → autophagosomes accumulate.
      m.fusion = 0.1; m.escrt = 1.1; break;
    case "pi3k_inhibitor":
      m.escrt = 0.7; m.autophagy = 0.7; m.lipids = 0.5; break;
    case "sterol_depletion":
      m.endocytosis = 0.7; m.escrt = 0.8; m.lipids = 0.3; m.exosomeTopology = 0.18; break;
    case "ap4_escrt_double":
      m.ap4Sorting = 0.05; m.escrt = 0.05; m.autophagy = 0.4; m.ros = 1.5; m.exosomeTopology = 0.45; break;
    case "ap4_dominant_negative":
      // Corrected: same topology risk as AP-4 KO (0.15). A DN sequesters cargo
      // but adds no mechanism for membrane-scission/topology errors beyond the KO
      // (see model-assumptions section).
      m.ap4Sorting = 0.3; m.autophagy = 0.7; m.npr1Nuclear = 0.7; m.exosomeTopology = 0.15; break;
  }
  return m;
}

// Light pathogen-specific modifiers (illustrative). The virus co-opts
// autophagy/endomembrane flux (potyvirus 6K2/VPg), fungi mainly drive ROS.
function getPathogenModifiers(type) {
  const p = { ros: 1, autophagy: 1, escrt: 1 };
  switch (type) {
    case "magnaporthe":    p.ros = 1.3; break;
    case "colletotrichum": p.ros = 1.1; p.autophagy = 0.85; break;
    case "botrytis":       p.ros = 1.5; break;
    case "fusarium":       p.ros = 1.2; break;
    case "potyvirus":      p.autophagy = 1.4; p.escrt = 1.2; break;
  }
  return p;
}
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false

// ============================================================================
// SIMULATION GENERATOR
// ============================================================================

simulation = {
  // Restart cleanly whenever controls change
  const dt = 0.1;
  let s = {
    pm: 100, ee: 0, mvb: 0, auto: 0, vac: 0,
    ros: 0, mapk: 0, callose: 0,
    pi3p_ee: 2.5, pi3p_mvb: 5.0,
    atg9_tgn: 100, npr1_nucleus: 0, cbp60g_nucleus: 0,
    exo_normal: 0, exo_reversed: 0,
    time: 0
  };
  let hist = [];
  let t = 0;

  const params = {
    receptor_activation: 0.5, internalization: 0.3,
    ubiquitination: 0.4, deubiquitination: 0.1, recycling: 0.2,
    escrt_recruitment: 0.25, ilv_formation: 0.35, mvb_fusion: 0.15,
    atg_activation: 0.1, autophagosome_formation: 0.2, autophagy_flux: 0.12,
    pi3p_production: 0.4, pi3p_consumption: 0.2,
    vacuolar_degradation: 0.08
  };

  while (true) {
    if (isRunning && t < timeMax) {
      const mo = getPerturbationModifiers(perturbation.value);
      const pmod = getPathogenModifiers(pathogen.value);
      const rv = response.value;

      // --- First-order mass-action fluxes (each proportional to dt exactly once) ---
      const internalized = s.pm  * params.internalization    * dt * mo.endocytosis;
      const recycled     = s.ee  * params.recycling          * dt * mo.recycling;
      // ESCRT+ubiquitin sorting of endosomal cargo into ILVs of the MVB
      const ilv_form     = s.ee  * params.escrt_recruitment  * dt * mo.escrt * mo.ubiquitination;
      // MVB→vacuole delivery of ILVs
      const mvb_fused    = s.mvb * params.mvb_fusion         * dt * mo.fusion;
      // autophagosome formation (ATG machinery) and autophagosome→vacuole fusion
      const auto_induced = s.ee  * params.atg_activation     * dt * mo.autophagy * pmod.autophagy;
      const auto_flux    = s.auto * params.autophagy_flux    * dt * mo.fusion;

      // --- Signalling (phenomenological, pathogen-modulated) ---
      const ros_t   = t < 5  ? Math.exp(-t / 2) * 100 * mo.ros * pmod.ros : s.ros  * 0.95;
      const mapk_t  = t < 10 ? (1 - Math.exp(-t / 3)) * 80 * mo.ros : s.mapk * 0.98;
      const cal_t   = t < 20 ? ros_t * 0.3 : s.callose * 0.95;

      // --- PI(3)P: produced on endosomes, consumed by ILV formation (ESCRT) ---
      const pi3p_ee_new  = Math.max(0.05, s.pi3p_ee  + 0.04 * dt * mo.lipids - 0.020 * ilv_form);
      const pi3p_mvb_new = Math.max(0.05, s.pi3p_mvb + 0.03 * dt * mo.lipids - 0.030 * ilv_form);

      // --- AP-4 cargo sorting; ATG9 backs up at the TGN when AP-4 is impaired ---
      const atg9_sorted   = s.atg9_tgn * 0.04 * dt * mo.ap4Sorting;
      const atg9_backlog  = (1 - mo.ap4Sorting) * 2.5 * dt;
      const npr1_import   = (rv === "eti" ? 0.30 : 0.12) * dt * mo.npr1Nuclear * mo.ap4Sorting;
      const cbp60g_import = 0.15 * dt * mo.cbp60gNuclear * mo.ap4Sorting;

      // --- Exosomes: reversed-topology fraction = intrinsic perturbation risk,
      //     amplified by membrane stress from CARGO BACKLOG. Keyed off accumulated
      //     cargo (not ESCRT flux or PI(3)P), so aberrant vesiculation under an
      //     ESCRT-deficient background (e.g. ap4+escrt double KO) still produces
      //     reversed exosomes — matching the "aberrant ILV formation without
      //     functional ESCRT" mechanism. ---
      const backlog       = Math.min(2, (s.ee + s.mvb) / 40);   // 0..2 membrane stress
      const exo_rev_gain  = mo.exosomeTopology * (0.5 + backlog) * 0.8 * dt;
      const exo_norm_gain = (1 - mo.exosomeTopology) * s.mvb * 0.04 * dt;

      s = {
        pm:             Math.max(0, s.pm   - internalized + recycled),
        ee:             Math.max(0, s.ee   + internalized - recycled - ilv_form - auto_induced),
        mvb:            Math.max(0, s.mvb  + ilv_form - mvb_fused),
        auto:           Math.max(0, s.auto + auto_induced - auto_flux),
        vac:            s.vac + mvb_fused + auto_flux,
        ros:            Math.max(0, ros_t),
        mapk:           Math.max(0, mapk_t),
        callose:        Math.max(0, cal_t),
        pi3p_ee:        pi3p_ee_new,
        pi3p_mvb:       pi3p_mvb_new,
        atg9_tgn:       Math.max(20, s.atg9_tgn - atg9_sorted + atg9_backlog),
        npr1_nucleus:   Math.max(0, Math.min(50, s.npr1_nucleus + npr1_import)),
        cbp60g_nucleus: Math.max(0, Math.min(30, s.cbp60g_nucleus + cbp60g_import)),
        exo_normal:     Math.max(0, s.exo_normal  + exo_norm_gain),
        exo_reversed:   Math.max(0, s.exo_reversed + exo_rev_gain),
        time: t
      };
      t = parseFloat((t + dt).toFixed(2));

      if (Math.round(t * 10) % 5 === 0) {
        hist = [...hist.slice(-800), { ...s }];
      }
    }

    yield { state: s, history: hist, time: t };
    await Promises.delay(30);
  }
}

simState   = simulation.state   ?? {}
simHistory = simulation.history ?? []
simTime    = simulation.time    ?? 0
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false

// ============================================================================
// PATHOLOGY DETECTION
// ============================================================================

pathologies = {
  const s = simState;
  const t = simTime;
  const alerts = [];
  // Failed receptor downregulation (impaired endocytosis) → receptors persist at PM
  if (s.pm > 70 && t > 30)
    alerts.push({ level: "warning", title: "Impaired receptor downregulation", msg: "PM receptors not cleared — risk of chronic signalling" });
  // Endosomal / MVB overload (impaired sorting or fusion)
  if (s.ee > 50 || s.mvb > 50)
    alerts.push({ level: "warning", title: "Endosomal overload", msg: "Cargo accumulating in EE/MVB — impaired sorting or fusion" });
  // Autophagosome accumulation without degradation (blocked fusion, e.g. bafilomycin)
  if (s.auto > 40 && t > 25)
    alerts.push({ level: "warning", title: "Autophagy flux blockade", msg: "Autophagosomes accumulate without vacuolar degradation" });
  // AP-4 deficiency signature: ATG9 backs up at the TGN
  if (s.atg9_tgn > 150 && t > 15)
    alerts.push({ level: "warning", title: "ATG9 accumulation at TGN", msg: "AP-4 deficiency → ATG9 mis-sorting → impaired autophagosome biogenesis" });
  // NPR1 fails to reach the nucleus despite signal (AP-4-dependent, predicted)
  if (s.npr1_nucleus < 3 && s.atg9_tgn > 130 && t > 30)
    alerts.push({ level: "warning", title: "NPR1 nuclear-import failure (predicted)", msg: "AP-4 deficiency → NPR1 mis-sorting → impaired SAR (hypothesis, cross-species inference)" });
  // PI(3)P depletion on MVB (membrane-identity loss; precondition for topology errors)
  if (s.pi3p_mvb < 1.0 && t > 15)
    alerts.push({ level: "warning", title: "PI(3)P depletion (MVB)", msg: "Loss of endosomal identity — ESCRT assembly compromised" });
  // Reversed-topology exosomes — the autoimmune-risk readout
  if (s.exo_reversed > 20)
    alerts.push({ level: "alert", title: "⚠ MEMBRANE TOPOLOGY INVERSION", msg: "Reversed-topology exosomes accumulating — cytoplasmic epitopes exposed → potential autoimmune response" });
  return alerts;
}
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false

// ============================================================================
// VISUALISATIONS
// ============================================================================

// Tidy-long helpers
traffLong = simHistory.flatMap(d => [
  { time: d.time, variable: "Plasma membrane", value: d.pm   },
  { time: d.time, variable: "Early endosome",  value: d.ee   },
  { time: d.time, variable: "MVB",             value: d.mvb  },
  { time: d.time, variable: "Vacuole",         value: d.vac  }
])

sigLong = simHistory.flatMap(d => [
  { time: d.time, variable: "ROS",  value: d.ros  },
  { time: d.time, variable: "MAPK", value: d.mapk }
])

pi3pLong = simHistory.flatMap(d => [
  { time: d.time, variable: "PI(3)P — EE",  value: d.pi3p_ee  },
  { time: d.time, variable: "PI(3)P — MVB", value: d.pi3p_mvb }
])

ap4Long = simHistory.flatMap(d => [
  { time: d.time, variable: "ATG9 (TGN)",     value: d.atg9_tgn       },
  { time: d.time, variable: "NPR1 (nucleus)", value: d.npr1_nucleus    },
  { time: d.time, variable: "CBP60g (nucleus)",value: d.cbp60g_nucleus },
  { time: d.time, variable: "Reversed exo.",  value: d.exo_reversed   }
])
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false

Plot.plot({
  title: "Trafficking Dynamics",
  width: 900, height: 280,
  x: { label: "Time (min)" },
  y: { label: "Count" },
  color: { legend: true },
  marks: [
    Plot.line(traffLong, { x: "time", y: "value", stroke: "variable", tip: true }),
    Plot.ruleY([0])
  ]
})
```
*Compartment pool sizes over time (arbitrary units): plasma-membrane receptors
are internalised into the early endosome and delivered via the MVB to the
vacuole. At steady state degradation balances production; perturbations shift the
balance — e.g. impaired ESCRT stalls cargo in the EE/MVB.*

```{ojs}
//| echo: false

Plot.plot({
  title: "Immune Signalling Output",
  width: 900, height: 240,
  x: { label: "Time (min)" },
  y: { label: "Activation level" },
  color: { legend: true },
  marks: [
    Plot.line(sigLong, { x: "time", y: "value", stroke: "variable", tip: true }),
    Plot.ruleY([0])
  ]
})
```
*Phenomenological immune-signalling outputs (ROS, MAPK): a fast transient rise
after perception followed by decay. Amplitude scales with the ROS modifier;
blocked receptor degradation prolongs the response.*

```{ojs}
//| echo: false

Plot.plot({
  title: "Phosphoinositide Dynamics",
  width: 900, height: 240,
  x: { label: "Time (min)" },
  y: { label: "PI(3)P concentration (μM)" },
  color: { legend: true },
  marks: [
    Plot.line(pi3pLong, { x: "time", y: "value", stroke: "variable", tip: true }),
    Plot.ruleY([0])
  ]
})
```
*Phosphoinositide pools on the early endosome and MVB (μM). PI(3)P is consumed by
ILV formation; a fall below ~1 μM on the MVB flags loss of endosomal identity and
is the precondition for the membrane-topology defects tracked below.*

```{ojs}
//| echo: false

Plot.plot({
  title: "AP-4 Cargo Panel",
  width: 900, height: 260,
  x: { label: "Time (min)" },
  y: { label: "Level" },
  color: { legend: true },
  marks: [
    Plot.line(ap4Long, { x: "time", y: "value", stroke: "variable", tip: true }),
    Plot.ruleY([0])
  ]
})
```
*AP-4-dependent cargo readouts: ATG9 at the TGN (rises when AP-4 is impaired — the
mis-sorting signature), nuclear NPR1/CBP60g (fall when AP-4 sorting drops), and
reversed-topology exosomes (the autoimmune-risk readout).*

```{ojs}
//| echo: false

// ============================================================================
// LIVE STATUS CARDS
// ============================================================================

html`<div style="
  display: grid;
  grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fill, minmax(140px, 1fr));
  gap: 10px; margin: 20px 0;">

  ${[
    { label: "Time (min)",     val: simTime?.toFixed(1),              color: "#6b7280", bg: "#f3f4f6" },
    { label: "PM Receptors",   val: simState.pm?.toFixed(1),          color: "#10b981", bg: "#f0fdf4" },
    { label: "EE Cargo",       val: simState.ee?.toFixed(1),          color: "#3b82f6", bg: "#eff6ff" },
    { label: "MVB ILVs",       val: simState.mvb?.toFixed(1),         color: "#f59e0b", bg: "#fef3c7" },
    { label: "Vacuole",        val: simState.vac?.toFixed(1),         color: "#ef4444", bg: "#fef2f2" },
    { label: "ROS",            val: simState.ros?.toFixed(1),         color: "#f43f5e", bg: "#fef2f2" },
    { label: "MAPK",           val: simState.mapk?.toFixed(1),        color: "#f43f5e", bg: "#fef2f2" },
    { label: "ATG9 TGN",       val: simState.atg9_tgn?.toFixed(1),   color: "#14b8a6", bg: "#f0fdfa" },
    { label: "NPR1 nucleus",   val: simState.npr1_nucleus?.toFixed(1),color: "#ec4899", bg: "#fdf2f8" },
    { label: "Reversed exo.",  val: simState.exo_reversed?.toFixed(2),color: "#dc2626", bg: "#fef2f2" },
  ].map(c => `
    <div style="background:${c.bg}; padding:10px; border-radius:6px;
                border-left:4px solid ${c.color};">
      <div style="font-size:11px; color:#555;">${c.label}</div>
      <div style="font-size:22px; font-weight:bold; color:${c.color};">${c.val ?? "—"}</div>
    </div>`
  ).join("")}
</div>`
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false

// Alerts panel
html`<div style="margin: 10px 0;">
  ${pathologies.length === 0
    ? `<div style="color:#10b981; font-weight:500; padding:10px;">✓ No pathological states detected</div>`
    : pathologies.map(a => `
        <div style="
          background:${a.level === 'alert' ? '#fef2f2' : '#fef3c7'};
          border:2px solid ${a.level === 'alert' ? '#ef4444' : '#f59e0b'};
          border-radius:6px; padding:12px; margin-bottom:8px;">
          <div style="font-weight:bold; color:${a.level === 'alert' ? '#dc2626' : '#d97706'};">
            ${a.title}
          </div>
          <div style="font-size:13px; color:${a.level === 'alert' ? '#b91c1c' : '#92400e'};">
            ${a.msg}
          </div>
        </div>`
      ).join("")
  }
</div>`
```

## Receptor fate and the PTI/ETI dichotomy

The compartment model above encodes a single decision that recurs throughout
plant immunity: once an activated receptor is internalised, is it **recycled**
back to the plasma membrane (sustaining signalling) or **committed to
degradation** (attenuating it)? Ubiquitination is the switch — K63-linked chains
route cargo into the ESCRT/MVB pathway, from where it is delivered either to the
vacuole (degradation) or, via NBR1, to selective autophagy [@spallek2013;
@ustun2018].

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-receptor-fate
%%| fig-cap: "Receptor-fate decision tree. Ubiquitination status routes an internalised receptor between recycling (sustained PTI) and ESCRT-mediated degradation, which in turn branches into vacuolar turnover or selective autophagy."
flowchart TD
  A[PRR activation<br/>MAMP/PAMP perception] --> B{Ubiquitinated?}
  B -- No / low --> C[Recycling to PM]
  C --> D[Sustained PTI signalling]
  B -- Yes, K63-Ub --> E[ESCRT sorting into MVB/ILVs]
  E --> F{Degradation route}
  F -- MVB --> G[Vacuolar degradation]
  G --> H[PTI attenuation]
  F -- Autophagy --> I[Selective autophagy<br/>NBR1-mediated]
  I --> J[ETI / HR regulation]
```

The two branches of immunity differ systematically in how they use this
trafficking machinery. Pattern-triggered immunity (PTI), initiated by
surface-localised pattern-recognition receptors (PRRs), is fast and relies
heavily on endocytosis and recycling; effector-triggered immunity (ETI),
initiated by intracellular NLR receptors, is slower, autophagy-dominant, and
culminates in the hypersensitive response (HR):

| Feature | PTI | ETI |
|---|---|---|
| Receptors | PRRs (plasma membrane) | NLRs (cytoplasm) |
| Activation | MAMP/PAMP | Effector (direct/indirect) |
| Kinetics | Fast (minutes) | Slower (hours) |
| Ubiquitination | PUB12/PUB13 | PUB22/PUB23 |
| Trafficking | Heavy endocytosis/recycling | Autophagy-dominant |
| ESCRT role | Signal attenuation | Positive *and* negative |
| Autophagy | Minor | Major (selective degradation) |
| Output | Basal resistance | HR + resistance |
| Amplitude | Moderate | Strong/hypersensitive |
| Duration | Transient | Prolonged |
| Key adaptors | TOM1, VPS23 | NBR1, TSPO |
| Membrane flux | High PM turnover | Organelle remodelling |
| Cell-death programme | No | Yes (PCD/HR) |

## Pathogen effector strategies

Because trafficking controls the amplitude and duration of immune signalling,
pathogen effectors repeatedly target it. The recurrent strategies fall into six
classes — each intercepting a different step of the receptor life-cycle mapped
above (effectors are illustrative examples from bacterial and oomycete systems):

| # | Strategy | Example effectors | Host targets | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Direct receptor suppression | AvrPto, AvrPtoB | FLS2, BAK1, CERK1 | Block receptor activation or promote its degradation |
| 2 | Endocytic disruption | HopM1, HopQ1 | ARA7 (Rab), RabF2a | Prevent endosome maturation/trafficking |
| 3 | SNARE inhibition | RXLR effectors, HopO1-1 | VTI12, SYP61 | Block vesicle fusion and recycling |
| 4 | Autophagy manipulation | AVR-Pik, AvrRps4 | NBR1, ATG8 | Prevent selective autophagy of NLRs |
| 5 | Ubiquitination hijacking | AvrPtoB (RING), Avr3a | PUB E3 ligases, CMPG1 | Act as an E3, or inhibit host ubiquitination |
| 6 | Guard-model trigger (ETI) | AvrRpm1, AvrRpt2 | RIN4 (guard) | Modify a guard protein, triggering NLR activation |

The evolutionary logic is an arms race: pathogens secrete effectors to suppress
PTI at multiple trafficking steps; plants evolve NLRs that detect those effectors
(or the modifications they make to guarded host proteins) and mount ETI. Because
NLR trafficking itself tunes ETI amplitude, and autophagy plays the dual role of
both suppressing PTI and regulating ETI, the balance between degradation and
recycling ultimately sets immune homeostasis.

The network below places these players together: plant proteins are coloured by
functional class and pathogen effectors (red) are joined to their host targets by
dashed **suppression** edges. It is a curated, literature-grounded map (hover a
node for its class; drag to rearrange), not an exhaustive interactome.

```{r effector-network}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| fig-cap: "Immune receptor-trafficking network with pathogen-effector targets. Plant nodes coloured by functional class; dashed red edges mark effector suppression of a host target. Curated from the interactions described in this chapter."
#| column: page
library(visNetwork)

nodes <- tibble::tribble(
  ~id,        ~group,
  "FLS2","PRR", "EFR","PRR", "CERK1","PRR", "BAK1","PRR",
  "BIK1","Kinase", "MAPKKK5","Kinase", "MKK4","Kinase", "MPK3","Kinase",
  "PUB12","E3 ligase", "PUB13","E3 ligase", "PUB22","E3 ligase", "CMPG1","E3 ligase",
  "CHC1","Endocytic", "AP2M","Endocytic", "DRP2A","Endocytic", "TOM1","Endocytic",
  "ARA6","Rab GTPase", "ARA7","Rab GTPase", "RabF2a","Rab GTPase",
  "VPS23","ESCRT", "VPS37","ESCRT", "VPS4","ESCRT", "FREE1","ESCRT",
  "NBR1","Autophagy", "ATG8","Autophagy", "ATG9","Autophagy", "AP4M","Autophagy",
  "VTI12","SNARE", "SYP61","SNARE",
  "RPS2","NLR", "SNC1","NLR", "RIN4","NLR",
  "AvrPto","Effector", "AvrPtoB","Effector", "HopM1","Effector",
  "AVR-Pik","Effector", "Avr3a","Effector", "AvrRpt2","Effector")
nodes$label <- nodes$id
pal <- c(PRR = "#7fb3d5", Kinase = "#82e0aa", `E3 ligase` = "#f5b7b1",
         Endocytic = "#f0b27a", `Rab GTPase` = "#a9dfbf", ESCRT = "#f9e79f",
         Autophagy = "#d2b4de", SNARE = "#f7dc6f", NLR = "#aed6f1",
         Effector = "#c0392b")
nodes$color <- pal[nodes$group]
nodes$shape <- ifelse(nodes$group == "Effector", "triangle", "dot")

edges <- tibble::tribble(
  ~from,     ~to,      ~type,
  "FLS2","BAK1","complex", "EFR","BAK1","complex", "CERK1","BAK1","complex",
  "FLS2","BIK1","phospho", "BAK1","BIK1","phospho", "BIK1","MAPKKK5","activation",
  "MAPKKK5","MKK4","phospho", "MKK4","MPK3","phospho",
  "FLS2","PUB12","ubiquitin", "FLS2","PUB13","ubiquitin", "PUB22","NBR1","ubiquitin",
  "FLS2","CHC1","complex", "CHC1","AP2M","complex", "AP2M","DRP2A","complex",
  "FLS2","TOM1","complex", "TOM1","VPS23","complex", "VPS23","VPS37","complex",
  "VPS37","VPS4","complex", "FREE1","VPS23","complex", "CHC1","ARA6","complex",
  "ARA6","ARA7","activation", "ARA7","RabF2a","activation",
  "NBR1","ATG8","complex", "AP4M","ATG9","complex", "ATG9","ATG8","activation",
  "TOM1","VTI12","complex", "VTI12","SYP61","complex",
  "RPS2","RIN4","complex", "SNC1","NBR1","complex",
  "AvrPto","FLS2","suppress", "AvrPtoB","BAK1","suppress", "AvrPtoB","FLS2","suppress",
  "HopM1","ARA7","suppress", "AVR-Pik","NBR1","suppress", "Avr3a","CMPG1","suppress",
  "AvrRpt2","RIN4","suppress")
edges$dashes <- edges$type == "suppress"
ecol <- c(complex = "#5d6d7e", phospho = "#e74c3c", activation = "#229954",
          ubiquitin = "#8e44ad", suppress = "#c0392b")
edges$color <- ecol[edges$type]

visNetwork(nodes, edges, width = "100%", height = "620px") |>
  visEdges(arrows = list(to = list(enabled = TRUE, scaleFactor = 0.5)),
           smooth = FALSE) |>
  visGroups() |>
  visOptions(highlightNearest = list(enabled = TRUE, degree = 1, hover = TRUE),
             selectedBy = "group") |>
  visLegend(width = 0.15, position = "right", main = "Class") |>
  visPhysics(stabilization = TRUE,
             barnesHut = list(gravitationalConstant = -8000, springLength = 120)) |>
  visLayout(randomSeed = 42)
```

## Model assumptions & calculations

The simulation is a **deterministic compartment model** integrated by explicit
Euler steps (Δt = 0.1 min). State variables are pool sizes (arbitrary units)
across compartments: plasma membrane (PM), early endosome (EE), MVB/ILVs,
autophagosome, vacuole, TGN (ATG9), nucleus (NPR1, CBP60g) and secreted exosomes
(normal vs reversed topology), plus PI(3)P on EE and MVB and the signalling
outputs ROS/MAPK/callose.

**Kinetics.** Transport steps are **first-order mass action**: each flux is the
product of a source pool, a rate constant, and the relevant perturbation ($m$)
and pathogen ($p$) modifiers. Writing the compartment pools as
$[\mathrm{PM}], [\mathrm{EE}], [\mathrm{MVB}], [\mathrm{Auto}], [\mathrm{Vac}]$,
the core system integrated by explicit Euler ($\Delta t = 0.1$) is

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d[\mathrm{PM}]}{dt}   &= -k_{\text{int}}\,m_{\text{endo}}\,[\mathrm{PM}] + k_{\text{rec}}\,m_{\text{rec}}\,[\mathrm{EE}]\\[2pt]
\frac{d[\mathrm{EE}]}{dt}   &= k_{\text{int}}m_{\text{endo}}[\mathrm{PM}] - k_{\text{rec}}m_{\text{rec}}[\mathrm{EE}] - \underbrace{k_{\text{ILV}}m_{\text{escrt}}m_{\text{ub}}[\mathrm{EE}]}_{J_{\text{ILV}}} - k_{\text{atg}}m_{\text{auto}}p_{\text{auto}}[\mathrm{EE}]\\[2pt]
\frac{d[\mathrm{MVB}]}{dt}  &= J_{\text{ILV}} - k_{\text{fus}}\,m_{\text{fus}}\,[\mathrm{MVB}]\\[2pt]
\frac{d[\mathrm{Auto}]}{dt} &= k_{\text{atg}}m_{\text{auto}}p_{\text{auto}}[\mathrm{EE}] - k_{\text{flux}}\,m_{\text{fus}}\,[\mathrm{Auto}]\\[2pt]
\frac{d[\mathrm{Vac}]}{dt}  &= k_{\text{fus}}m_{\text{fus}}[\mathrm{MVB}] + k_{\text{flux}}m_{\text{fus}}[\mathrm{Auto}].
\end{aligned}
$$

ESCRT sorting into ILVs ($J_{\text{ILV}}$) requires **both** ubiquitination and
ESCRT function, hence the product $m_{\text{escrt}}m_{\text{ub}}$. Rate constants
($k \approx 0.1$–$0.5\ \text{min}^{-1}$; see table) are **order-of-magnitude
estimates** from plant endomembrane/autophagy studies, **not fitted to data** —
absolute values are illustrative and only relative trends are meaningful.
Signalling outputs are phenomenological, e.g.
$[\mathrm{ROS}](t) = 100\,m_{\text{ros}}p_{\text{ros}}\,e^{-t/2}$ for $t<5$ min
and $0.95\,[\mathrm{ROS}]$ decay thereafter (MAPK, callose analogous).

**PI(3)P coupling.** PI(3)P is produced on endosomes and **consumed in proportion
to ILV formation**, with a floor at $0.05$:

$$
\frac{d[\mathrm{PI3P}]_{\mathrm{EE}}}{dt} = a\,m_{\text{lip}} - b\,J_{\text{ILV}},
\qquad [\mathrm{PI3P}] \ge 0.05,
$$

(and analogously on the MVB). Excessive ILV formation therefore depletes PI(3)P,
which (a) impairs further ESCRT assembly and (b) raises the membrane-stress term.

**AP-4 cargo.** ATG9 export and NPR1/CBP60g nuclear import scale with AP-4
function $m_{\text{ap4}}$; when AP-4 is impaired, ATG9 backs up at the TGN
($+c\,(1-m_{\text{ap4}})$) and nuclear import falls:
$\frac{d[\mathrm{NPR1}_n]}{dt} = r_{\text{resp}}\,m_{\text{npr1}}\,m_{\text{ap4}}$,
with $r_{\text{resp}}$ higher under ETI than PTI.

**Topology-reversal heuristic.** Reversed-topology exosomes accumulate as

$$
\frac{d[\mathrm{exo}_{\text{rev}}]}{dt} = m_{\text{topo}}\,\sigma\,k_{\text{exo}},
\qquad
\sigma = 0.5 + \min\!\left(2,\ \frac{[\mathrm{EE}]+[\mathrm{MVB}]}{40}\right),
$$

where the *intrinsic risk* $m_{\text{topo}}$ is set per perturbation from
mechanistic reasoning and the *stress* factor $\sigma$ rises with cargo backlog.
Crucially this term is **decoupled from normal ESCRT flux**, so aberrant
vesiculation (e.g. the AP-4+ESCRT double KO) still generates reversed exosomes.
Intrinsic-risk values:

| Perturbation | Reversed-topology risk | Basis |
|---|---|---|
| AP-4 + ESCRT double KO | 0.45 | Synergistic failure; aberrant ILV formation without ESCRT |
| VPS4-DN | 0.35 | Direct ESCRT-III disassembly defect (strongest single driver) |
| Sterol depletion | 0.18 | Impaired membrane curvature/scission |
| AP-4 KO | 0.15 | **Indirect only** (autophagy→ESCRT stress) |
| AP-4-DN | 0.15 | **Same as KO** — a DN adds no scission/topology mechanism |
| ESCRT KO | 0.12 | Impaired ILV formation |

The last two rows encode a deliberate correction: **AP-4 is a cargo adaptor, not
part of the scission/curvature machinery**, so an AP-4 dominant-negative has no
mechanistic route to *higher* topology risk than the knockout — both are set to
0.15, driven indirectly via autophagy→ESCRT stress.

**Rate constants.** The symbols above take these illustrative values (per the
`params` object in the simulation code):

| Symbol | Meaning | Value (min⁻¹) |
|---|---|---|
| $k_{\text{int}}$ | receptor internalisation | 0.30 |
| $k_{\text{rec}}$ | endosome → PM recycling | 0.20 |
| $k_{\text{ILV}}$ | ESCRT/ILV sorting | 0.25 |
| $k_{\text{fus}}$ | MVB → vacuole fusion | 0.15 |
| $k_{\text{atg}}$ | autophagosome induction | 0.10 |
| $k_{\text{flux}}$ | autophagosome → vacuole flux | 0.12 |
| $a$ | PI(3)P production | 0.04 |
| $b$ | PI(3)P consumption per ILV flux | 0.020 (EE) / 0.030 (MVB) |
| $k_9,\ c$ | ATG9 TGN export / backlog | 0.04 / 2.5 |
| $r_{\text{resp}}$ | NPR1 nuclear import | 0.12 (PTI) / 0.30 (ETI) |
| $k_{\text{exo}}$ | reversed-exosome rate | 0.80 |

**Caveats.** Parameters are illustrative; plant AP-4 cargo beyond ATG9 (NPR1,
CBP60g) is predicted from cross-species homology (flagged *predicted*); pathogen
modifiers are light and illustrative (the potyvirus setting reflects documented
autophagy/endomembrane co-option, not a fitted infection model).

# Extended analysis: AP-4, TEPSIN and cargo missorting

The simulation foregrounds AP-4 because it is the least-studied of the four
adaptor-protein complexes yet, we argue, a plausible integration point between
membrane trafficking and immune signalling. This section sets out the
mechanistic reasoning and the (largely hypothetical) cargo assignments that
motivate the model's AP-4 arm.

::: {.callout-important appearance="simple"}
Much of what follows is **predictive**: it extrapolates from well-characterised
mammalian AP-4 biology and from sorting-motif analysis to plant proteins that
have **not** been experimentally validated as AP-4 cargo. Established facts
(ATG9A as AP-4 cargo in mammals; VSR1/BOR1 as AP-4 cargo in *Arabidopsis*;
AP-4's requirement for the hypersensitive response) are cited; predictions are
labelled as such and presented as testable hypotheses, not conclusions.
:::

## AP-4: the overlooked trafficking pathway

Adaptor protein complex 4 (AP-4) is a heterotetramer — subunits β4 (AP4B,
At5g11490), ε (AP4E, At5g09420), μ4 (AP4M, At4g24550) and σ4 (AP4S, At3g13420)
in *Arabidopsis*. Unlike AP-1/2/3, AP-4 operates **without a clathrin coat** and
mediates export from the *trans*-Golgi network (TGN) to endosomal and
autophagy-related compartments [@hirst2013; @fuji2016].

| Complex | Location | Coat | Function | Knockout phenotype |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| AP-1 | TGN/endosomes | Clathrin | TGN–endosome, secretion | Embryo-lethal (mammals) |
| AP-2 | Plasma membrane | Clathrin | Endocytosis | Lethal (plants/mammals) |
| AP-3 | TGN/endosomes | None | Vacuole/lysosome | Hermansky–Pudlak (mammals) |
| **AP-4** | **TGN** | **None** | **TGN → endosome/autophagy** | **Neurodegeneration (mammals); subtle (plants)** |

The compartments AP-4 connects, and the lipid identities that mark them, are
summarised below; AP-4 acts at the TGN, exporting cargo (notably ATG9) toward
endosomal and autophagy compartments (dashed arrow).

```{=html}
<figure style="margin:1em 0;">
<svg viewBox="0 0 720 430" width="100%" style="max-width:720px;font-family:sans-serif;" role="img" aria-label="Plant cell trafficking compartment map">
  <rect x="6" y="6" width="708" height="418" rx="18" fill="#fbfaf5" stroke="#8a7f5c" stroke-width="3"/>
  <rect x="12" y="12" width="696" height="406" rx="14" fill="none" stroke="#f3c9c0" stroke-width="4"/>
  <text x="24" y="34" font-size="13" fill="#8a7f5c" font-style="italic">plasma membrane (PC, PE, PI, PS; sterols)</text>
  <!-- nucleus -->
  <circle cx="150" cy="150" r="62" fill="#e8daf2" stroke="#7d5ba6" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="150" y="155" font-size="14" text-anchor="middle" fill="#4a2f6b" font-weight="bold">Nucleus</text>
  <!-- ER -->
  <ellipse cx="150" cy="270" rx="70" ry="30" fill="#d6ecec" stroke="#4a9a9a" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="150" y="274" font-size="12" text-anchor="middle" fill="#2e6b6b">ER · PC/PE/PI, low sterol</text>
  <!-- Golgi/TGN -->
  <g>
    <rect x="330" y="120" width="120" height="12" rx="6" fill="#f4d35e"/>
    <rect x="335" y="138" width="110" height="12" rx="6" fill="#f4d35e"/>
    <rect x="340" y="156" width="100" height="12" rx="6" fill="#f4d35e"/>
    <text x="390" y="188" font-size="12" text-anchor="middle" fill="#8a6d0a" font-weight="bold">TGN / Golgi</text>
    <text x="390" y="203" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle" fill="#8a6d0a">PI(4)P · AP-4 acts here</text>
  </g>
  <!-- early endosome -->
  <circle cx="560" cy="150" r="40" fill="#fde3cf" stroke="#d98841" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="560" y="148" font-size="12" text-anchor="middle" fill="#9a531a">Early</text>
  <text x="560" y="163" font-size="12" text-anchor="middle" fill="#9a531a">endosome</text>
  <text x="560" y="205" font-size="10" text-anchor="middle" fill="#9a531a">PI(3)P</text>
  <!-- MVB -->
  <circle cx="580" cy="270" r="34" fill="#f5c6a5" stroke="#c0651f" stroke-width="2"/>
  <circle cx="571" cy="264" r="5" fill="#c0651f"/><circle cx="588" cy="266" r="5" fill="#c0651f"/>
  <circle cx="578" cy="280" r="5" fill="#c0651f"/>
  <text x="580" y="320" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle" fill="#8a4413">MVB · PI(3,5)P₂</text>
  <!-- autophagosome -->
  <circle cx="440" cy="300" r="30" fill="#e2d0ee" stroke="#8e5bb5" stroke-width="2" stroke-dasharray="5 3"/>
  <text x="440" y="304" font-size="10" text-anchor="middle" fill="#5a3480">Autophago-</text>
  <text x="440" y="316" font-size="10" text-anchor="middle" fill="#5a3480">some</text>
  <!-- vacuole -->
  <ellipse cx="470" cy="380" rx="150" ry="34" fill="#dfe8f5" stroke="#3f6fa5" stroke-width="2"/>
  <text x="470" y="384" font-size="13" text-anchor="middle" fill="#274a70" font-weight="bold">Vacuole (degradation)</text>
  <!-- arrows -->
  <defs><marker id="ah" markerWidth="9" markerHeight="9" refX="7" refY="3" orient="auto"><path d="M0,0 L7,3 L0,6 Z" fill="#c0392b"/></marker>
        <marker id="ag" markerWidth="9" markerHeight="9" refX="7" refY="3" orient="auto"><path d="M0,0 L7,3 L0,6 Z" fill="#555"/></marker></defs>
  <!-- AP-4 TGN -> endosome (highlighted, dashed) -->
  <path d="M452,140 Q510,120 522,140" fill="none" stroke="#c0392b" stroke-width="2.5" stroke-dasharray="6 4" marker-end="url(#ah)"/>
  <text x="500" y="112" font-size="11" fill="#c0392b" font-weight="bold">AP-4 (ATG9)</text>
  <!-- EE -> MVB -->
  <path d="M565,190 Q575,225 578,234" fill="none" stroke="#555" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#ag)"/>
  <!-- MVB -> vacuole -->
  <path d="M575,304 Q540,335 500,350" fill="none" stroke="#555" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#ag)"/>
  <!-- autophagosome -> vacuole -->
  <path d="M440,330 Q450,350 460,346" fill="none" stroke="#8e5bb5" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#ag)"/>
</svg>
<figcaption style="font-size:12px;color:#555;">Plant-cell trafficking compartments and their marker lipids. AP-4 exports cargo (notably ATG9) from the TGN toward endosomal/autophagy compartments (red dashed arrow); ESCRT-marked MVBs and autophagosomes both deliver to the vacuole for degradation.</figcaption>
</figure>
```

AP-4 is understudied in plants precisely because it is *not* essential for
viability and gives subtle phenotypes — yet in mammals its loss causes a
hereditary spastic paraplegia ("AP-4 deficiency syndrome") traced to
missorting of its most conserved cargo, the autophagy lipid-scramblase **ATG9A**
[@mattera2017; @davies2018]. In *Arabidopsis*, AP-4 is experimentally
established to sort the tyrosine-motif cargoes VACUOLAR SORTING RECEPTOR 1 and
the borate transporter BOR1 via its µ subunit, and its loss abolishes the
hypersensitive cell-death response to avirulent bacteria [@fuji2016].

### Predicted plant AP-4 cargo

Mammalian AP-4 cargo (ATG9A, AMPA receptors, SLC17A5, SERINC1/3, δ-opioid
receptor, ADAM10) are transmembrane proteins bearing **[D/E]XXX[L/I/M]** or
**YXXΦ** sorting motifs and functioning in membrane remodelling, receptor
localisation, autophagosome formation or lipid transport. Screening plant
immune-relevant proteins for the same motifs yields a ranked set of candidate
cargo — **a bioinformatic prediction, not a validated interaction map**:

| Confidence | Candidate | Sorting motif(s) | Predicted missorting consequence |
|---|---|---|---|
| High | ATG9 (At2g31260) | multiple DXXXL/EXXXL in cytoplasmic loops | ATG9 accumulates at TGN → impaired autophagosome biogenesis |
| High | SYP4 SNAREs (SYP41/42/43) | (VAMP7-like, by homology) | ectopic TGN–endosome fusion |
| High | VTI11/VTI12 | (VAMP8-like) | failed autophagosome–vacuole fusion |
| Medium | **NPR1** (At1g64280) | DXXXL (234–238), EXXXM (456–460) | mis-localised NPR1 → impaired SAR (see below) |
| Medium | **CBP60g** (At5g26920) | YXXL (189–193), DXXXL (341–345) | dysregulated SA biosynthesis |
| Medium | NRAMP3 (At2g23150) | EIXXL (445–449) | iron mislocalisation → oxidative stress |
| Medium | PUB22/23 | membrane-associated E3s | failed NLR ubiquitination → NLR accumulation |
| Speculative | RabG3, cleaved PRR tails | — | impaired autophagosome fusion; chronic fragment signalling |

Only the ATG9 assignment rests on strong cross-species precedent [@mattera2017;
@davies2018]; the immune-regulator assignments (NPR1, CBP60g, PUB22/23) are the
novel, lower-confidence predictions that the callout above flags.

## TEPSIN: the AP-4 accessory protein

TEPSIN is an AP-4-interacting protein that stabilises the complex, enhances
membrane curvature and concentrates cargo prior to clathrin-independent scission
[@hirst2013]. A plant TEPSIN-like protein is present but uncharacterised
(*Arabidopsis* candidate At5g48795). We hypothesise it stabilises AP-4 under the
elevated trafficking demand of stress responses, predicting that *tepsin* mutants
phenocopy mild *ap4* defects and that TEPSIN–AP-4 association is enhanced during
infection. The mechanistically distinctive outcome of TEPSIN (or AP-4) loss is
not a clean autophagy block but a **dysfunctional** one:

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-tepsin-autophagy
%%| fig-cap: "Hypothesised consequence of TEPSIN/AP-4 loss: ATG9 mis-sorting yields structurally aberrant, cargo-incompetent autophagosomes rather than a complete autophagy block."
flowchart TD
  A[TEPSIN / AP-4 loss] --> B[ATG9 mis-sorted,<br/>accumulates at TGN]
  B --> C[Reduced ATG9 delivery to phagophore]
  C --> D[Impaired autophagosome formation]
  D --> E{ATG8 lipidation still occurs?}
  E -- Yes --> F[Aberrant autophagosomes:<br/>ATG8+ membranes, poor cargo recruitment]
  F --> G[Non-selective autophagy impaired]
  F --> H[Selective NBR1 autophagy compensatorily enhanced]
  F --> I[Chronic autophagy-stress response]
```

## NPR1 and CBP60g: trafficking hypotheses for immune regulators

NPR1, the master regulator of systemic acquired resistance (SAR), is held as
cytoplasmic oligomers that must be reduced to monomers — via thioredoxins and
S-nitrosylation at Cys156 — before nuclear import and coactivation of *PR* genes
[@mou2003; @tada2008; @kinkema2000]. The compartment in which monomerisation
occurs is not fully resolved. We propose (hypothesis) that AP-4 routes NPR1
oligomers through an endosomal compartment whose reducing environment promotes
monomerisation before retrograde delivery and nuclear import:

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-npr1-hypothesis
%%| fig-cap: "Hypothesised AP-4-dependent endosomal step in NPR1 activation (predicted, not established). AP-4 loss could either reduce nuclear NPR1 (loss of function) or, if the compartment normally sequesters NPR1, increase it (gain of function/autoimmunity)."
flowchart LR
  A[NPR1 oligomers<br/>cytoplasm] --> B[AP-4-mediated<br/>TGN sorting]
  B --> C[Endosomal compartment<br/>reducing environment]
  C --> D[Monomerisation]
  D --> E[Retrograde to cytosol]
  E --> F[Nuclear import → PR genes]
  B -. AP-4 loss .-> G[Missorted / sequestered NPR1]
  G --> H[Reduced SAR<br/>or constitutive autoimmunity]
```

This predicts that *ap4* mutants show altered NPR1 localisation and SA-response
kinetics, testable by *ap4 npr1* epistasis. Analogously, CBP60g — a
calcium-responsive transcription factor that activates the SA-biosynthesis gene
*ICS1* [@wang2009cbp60g] — may be held in a vesicular pool and released for rapid
perinuclear import upon MAMP-triggered Ca²⁺ influx, with AP-4 providing the
sorting step; *ap4* mutants would then show delayed CBP60g nuclear accumulation
and reduced SA after infection.

## Cargo missorting as a distinct pathology

The central hypothesis reframes AP-4 deficiency: the damaging consequence may be
not *failed degradation* but **aberrant localisation**, producing a signalling
imbalance. Missorting can create autophagy-resistant protein aggregates and
mislocalised signalling components — a route to chronic, autoimmune-like
activation that is mechanistically distinct from simple loss of function. Several
independent routes converge on this outcome, paralleling known plant autoimmune
mutants (e.g. ESCRT-defective *vps* alleles that fail to degrade activated
receptors; autophagy-deficient backgrounds that accumulate NLRs):

- **Failed receptor degradation** → receptor persistence at the PM → chronic MAPK.
- **NLR hyperaccumulation** in autophagy-deficient backgrounds → ETI without effector.
- **Persistent endosomal signalling platforms** when ESCRT sorting fails.
- **Lipid-identity imbalance** (PI(3)P/PI(3,5)P₂ dysregulation) → trafficking paralysis and, at the extreme, the membrane-topology inversion tracked by the simulation [@simon2016].
- **Cargo missorting → aggregates** that resist autophagy and trigger stress signalling.

These mechanisms are why the model reads out an *autoimmune-risk* signal
(reversed-topology exosomes) rather than a simple loss-of-signal, and why the
AP-4 arm — despite AP-4's subtle single-mutant phenotype — is worth modelling.

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## React/recharts version (not compatible with the OJS runtime)

The full React component (`PlantImmuneTrafficDashboard`) with recharts
visualisations is preserved in the project history for reference. To enable it,
either (a) host it as a standalone React app and embed via `<iframe>`, or
(b) move to a Shiny app (`shiny/immu_simu/`) with the same simulation logic.
:::
